reasoning: The document exhibits exceptional text extraction quality with a near-perfect composite score of 0.9973 and strong shingling metrics. However, programmatic structural detectors identified two major defects: a full-page image duplicating extracted text and lists improperly split across page breaks. Under the count-based routing rules, the presence of these two major programmatic defects mandates a routing to HUMAN_REVIEW.

concerns.description: Full-page image alongside extracted text (1 page(s))
concerns.analysis: suspect_pages=1. A scanned/screenshot page image duplicates content already present as text elements.

concerns.description: List broken by page break (2 instance(s)): p2→p3: list item ends “…arding air pollution. Comments were made”, continues as paragraph; p3→p4: list item ends “…,000 people are relying on water intakes”, continues as paragraph
concerns.analysis: split_list_count=2. Lists that span page breaks should be extracted as a single continuous structure. Split lists affect reading order and navigability for assistive technology.

The title 'MEMORANDUM' is clearly underlined in the source PDF. The output captures it as a heading but fails to retain the underline styling (via HTML tags), which constitutes a loss of visual emphasis. Note: The missing bolding on the list item lead-ins (e.g., 'Environmental:', 'Property Values:') is explicitly excluded from this check per instructions, but the missing underline is a valid finding.

concerns.description: Header/footer elements retained in output (1): State of North Carolina | Environmental Quality | Energy, Mi
concerns.analysis: header_footer_count=1. Header/footer text from extraction JSON is present in the rendered HTML — the renderer failed to strip it. This is a structural accessibility issue.

action_items: Investigate the suspect full-page image to ensure it does not create duplicative, inaccessible content alongside the extracted text
Merge the split lists spanning pages 2→3 and pages 3→4 into continuous list structures for proper assistive technology navigation
Restore the underline styling to the 'MEMORANDUM' heading if visually necessary
